mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 6 Series 630i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 630i engine typically costs £1,800–£8,500 supply only depending on generation. N52-era engines sit at the lower end (£1,800–£4,500), while B48 units run £3,500–£8,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Used E63 630i engines can be found from around £1,200 upwards. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 6 Series 630i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which of the four engine generations your 630i has. The N52 (E63/E64 pre-2007) is widely considered the most dependable choice in the 6 Series lineup. The N53 (E63/E64 LCI 2007–2010) has HPFP and injector issues. The N55 (F06/F12/F13 2011–2018) is a reliable turbo six but gaskets will need attention. The B48 (G32 2017–2020) is modern and efficient but still has oil and coolant leak concerns. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 6 Series 630i?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system failures (water pump, thermostat, expansion tank) and oil leaks (valve cover, oil filter housing) on the N52 and N53; HPFP and injector failure on the N53; oil leaks and HPFP issues on the N55; and oil/coolant leaks on the B48. The N52 is the most reliable overall.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 6 Series 630i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well-kept £6,000 E63 630i, a £4,000 recon is 67% of the car's value — borderline. Against a tired £4,500 example, the same recon exceeds our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. For F06 N55-engined cars (2011–2018), higher vehicle values (£10,000–£20,000) make replacement more viable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 6 Series 630i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: E63/E64 (2003–2007) is N52B30 (258 hp), E63/E64 LCI (2007–2010) is N53B30A (268 hp), F06/F12/F13 (2011–2018) is N55B30 (302–320 hp), G32 (2017–2020) is B48B20B (258 hp). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N53 HPFP, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The HPFP typically requires replacement rather than repair (£500–£1,500). Injectors often fail around the same time — when one injector fails, the others are often not far behind. Replacement requires proper coding by a specialist or BMW dealer.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current B48/N55 generations, the most common quote requests today. Older generations: see the Era Map above for N52 and N53-specific pricing, which runs lower. Used E63 630i engines can be found from around £1,200 upwards.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 6 Series 630i engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The N53's direct-injection system and N55's turbo plumbing require specialist knowledge.

cta.label: → Compare 6 Series 630i engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: N52/N53 Cooling System & Oil Leaks (E63/E64 2003–2010)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, steam from engine bay, low coolant warning; burning oil smell, visible oil on engine.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — age-related as plastic and gaskets degrade.
problems.repairCost: £200–£800 (water pump, thermostat, expansion tank); £300–£700 (valve cover gasket).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring coolant warnings can lead to head gasket failure.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the coolant warning comes on, pull over and let the engine cool.

problems.recommendation: The N52 and N53 are solid engines, but cooling system components are a known weakness — plastic thermostat housings, radiator end tanks, and water pump impellers become brittle with age. Oil leaks from the valve cover and oil filter housing gaskets are also common. The N53's electric water pump is prone to sudden failure with little warning. If you see any coolant warnings, have the system inspected immediately.

problems.title: N53 High-Pressure Fuel Pump & Injector Failure (E63/E64 LCI 2007–2010)
problems.symptoms: Long cranking, rough idle, loss of power, engine warning light, stalling, misfires.
problems.repairCost: £500–£1,500 (HPFP replacement); £1,200+ for a full set of injectors.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring symptoms can lead to further engine damage.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The N53's high-pressure fuel pump and piezo injectors are well-documented weak points. HPFP failure can cause stalling and poor performance. When one injector fails, the others are often not far behind. Replacement requires proper coding by a specialist or BMW dealer. Use high-octane fuel and avoid thrashing the car from cold to help mitigate issues.

problems.title: N55 Oil Leaks & HPFP Issues (F06/F12/F13 2011–2018)
problems.symptoms: Burning oil smell, visible oil on the engine, low oil level warnings; long cranking, rough idle.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, oil leaks can lead to oil starvation.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The N55 is a significant improvement over the N54, but it still suffers from oil leaks from the valve cover and oil filter housing gaskets. HPFP failures can also occur. The oil filter housing gasket leak is particularly critical — oil dripping onto the serpentine belt can cause belt failure and potential engine damage. If you notice a burning oil smell, have the gaskets inspected promptly.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 630i? Check These First
buyIf: * E63/E64 N52 with cooling system refreshed and gaskets replaced — the most dependable 6 Series choice
* E63/E64 N53 with HPFP and injectors replaced — but keep a repair fund
* F06/F12/F13 N55 with gaskets replaced and service history — reliable turbo six
* G32 B48 with full service history — modern and efficient
avoidIf: * 2007–2010 N53 with no HPFP or injector history — expensive repairs await
* Cooling system warnings on any generation — can indicate head gasket failure
* Poor service history on any generation — these engines are unforgiving of neglect
* N55 with oil filter housing gasket leak — can cause belt failure and engine damage

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 630i badge spans a remarkable range of engines — from the dependable N52 to the direct-injection N53, the turbocharged N55, and the modern B48. The N52 (pre-2007 E63/E64) is widely considered the most reliable choice in the 6 Series lineup. The N53 is best avoided unless you have a repair fund for HPFP and injectors. The N55 is a reliable turbo six but gaskets will need attention. The B48 (G32) is modern and efficient but still has oil and coolant leak concerns. With the 70% BMW scrap threshold, a £4,000 recon on a £6,000 E63 630i is 67% — borderline. On a sub-£5,000 example, think carefully.

cta.label: → Compare 6 Series 630i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 6 Series 630i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or ticking on startup (N52 lifter noise; N53/N55 timing chain)
* Service History — Including which engine era and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Coolant Check — Check level and condition — overheating kills these engines
* HPFP/Injector History — Any replacement on N53? Keep £1,200+ in reserve
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve cover and oil filter housing (N52/N55 common)
* Water Pump — Ask if the electric water pump has been replaced (N52/N53)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
